trajectory_summary: The call started with a recoverable legitimacy gap — Larry expected Tesla product, got Ion — and ended with that gap unresolved and compounded by scope creep, a missed roofing cross-sell, and a data-trust concession. The roof objection at 06:19 was a genuine but temporary barrier that an Ion roofing bridge would have converted into a separate revenue event; instead Jake surrendered the lead. The trajectory is consistently energy-leaking: each exchange left Larry with less trust and more reasons to disengage, until he closed the door on his own terms using the satellite-check criticism.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag 'no_interest / unqualified' is partially correct but imprecise. Larry is NOT disqualified — he is pre-qualified pending roof completion, and the roofing timeline is an Ion opportunity, not a dead end. A more accurate tag would be 'deferred_solar_pipeline / roofing_cross_sell_missed.' The call ended without a booking because Jake did not bridge to Ion's roofing team, not because Larry is unqualifiable.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Cross-sell bridge discipline — roof objection as roofing lead
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Yeah. I mean, you got our number, so you can give us a callback if, it is something that you're kinda trying to look into when, you do get that roof. Alright?
primary_coaching_focus.why: The roof-before-solar objection is one of the most common disqualifiers in this industry, and Ion's in-house roofing capability converts it from a dead end into an immediate revenue event plus a locked solar pipeline entry. Jake did not deploy this bridge — likely because he did not know it existed. Until Jake internalizes this move, every roof-objection call ends the same way: passive exit, zero revenue. The roofing bridge also preserves the deferred solar relationship; without it, Larry has no structural reason to call Ion back when his roof is done.

key_moments.quote: Hey, man. This is Jake with Ion. I was just giving you a call here because I saw that you requested some information on solar for your house.
key_moments.stewart_read: Jake's opening never deploys the Tesla-certified-installer framing the schema prescribes for Tesla-sourced leads. He introduces Ion as a generic solar company and references a form submission, not the Tesla referral. This is the original sin of the call — the legitimacy anchor that would have answered 'I'm not familiar with Ion' before Larry had to ask it. The Tesla framing (per intros.tesla) exists precisely to answer 'is this a scam?' in the first 30 seconds. Jake skipped it.

key_moments.quote: I think I have to have more trees taken down Oh, yeah. Before beforehand.
key_moments.stewart_read: Larry volunteers — unprompted — that he has already had trees removed and has eight more scheduled. This is a customer actively managing his property to make solar viable. It is the clearest enthusiasm signal on the call. Jake acknowledges it ('Okay') and pivots to address confirmation rather than anchoring on it. The correct move here is to reflect the signal back: Larry has been planning this for a while and is removing obstacles himself — that is a buyer orientation, not a browser orientation.

key_moments.quote: We we typically get the numbers from the lead sources. We don't always control what they, put out, unfortunately.
key_moments.stewart_read: Larry has just named 'false advertisement on Facebook' as the source of his information and implicitly accused Ion of being connected to it. Jake's response distances Ion from responsibility but does not repair the trust gap. The schema protocol for 'how did you get my number' calls for honest specificity: name the referral source cleanly and anchor Ion's legitimacy separately. Jake did neither — he issued a partial disclaimer and pivoted to the product. The result is that Larry's suspicion remains unaddressed and resurfaces at 07:21.

key_moments.quote: Is that something y'all do? It's something I think I could probably do myself, actually.
key_moments.stewart_read: Larry's ground-mount question is his peak curiosity moment — he has observed a neighbor's installation, identified a technical workaround for his east-west roof, and is actively problem-solving. This is an enthusiasm signal and a scope-creep trap simultaneously. The enthusiasm: Larry is genuinely interested in solar if the technical fit exists. The trap: Jake answered the question directly ('we actually don't do the ground mount systems anymore') instead of bridging it to the specialist who can actually address it. This triggered the roof-leak warranty debate that followed — a 90-second exchange Jake did not win.
